segments.s600-g113: Literally: 'every day hunt bad-doing his'. Persian omits the copula here, so the adjectival phrase 'بد کار' (= 'badly done' / 'ill-successful') predicates of 'نخچیر' ('hunt, game'). 'اوی' is the archaic third-person clitic meaning 'his' (or 'of him'), attaching to the whole noun phrase; word order is determiner + time ('همه روز') + noun ('نخچیر') + predicate ('بد کار') + possessive clitic ('اوی').
segments.s600-g114: 'دگر' here functions as 'other / besides / moreover' introducing an appositive list; there is no verb — the line simply enumerates items that occupy or characterize him: 'horse(s) and the field and polo and ball.' Each noun is joined by the coordinating 'و' ('and').
segments.s600-g105: گر is the conditional particle 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/adverbial time-word meaning 'now' or 'from now on'. نیرو is the direct object 'strength/power'. دهد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive ('gives' / 'may give') used in the protasis after گر. کردگار, 'the Creator/God', is the subject but placed after the verb for poetic inversion (normal prose order would be کردگار نیرو دهد).
segments.s600-g106: به کام دل ما is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/for the desire of our heart(s)' (کام = wish/desire; دل ما = 'our heart'). شود is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive/optative 'become' or 'may be'. روزگار is the theme/subject 'time(s)/fate/fortune' and is placed after the verb (another poetic inversion): literally 'becomes, the times'.
segments.s600-g107: برین is بر + این ('on/over/with these') functioning as a prepositional/demonstrative phrase. نیکویها is the plural-form noun 'good things/virtues' (نیکوی + -ها). فزایش is the noun 'increase'. بود is the copula in the past/remote sense ('there was' / 'was'), so the clause states an existential/predicative idea: 'there was an increase in these good qualities.'
segments.s600-g108: شما را uses را as the definite object/target marker attached to 'you' to indicate what the praise is about. بر ما here indicates the perspective/source ('by us' / 'on our part' — an older/poetic usage). ستایش is the noun 'praise'. بود again is the copula 'there was' / 'was', so the clause literally says 'there was praise of you by us' (i.e. 'you were praised by us' / 'you received our praise').
segments.s600-g109: همه شهر ایران is a determiner + noun phrase: همه (all) modifies شهر ایران (the cities of Iran). به گفتار اوی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'at/on his word' where به is the preposition 'at/on/to', گفتار is 'speech/word', and اوی is an archaic third-person possessive ('his') attached after the noun. The whole segment sets who went and why (they acted on his saying).
segments.s600-g110: برفتند is the simple past 3rd-person-plural 'they went/they departed' (رفتن), here describing the action of the cities' people. شادان‌دل and تازه‌روی are two adjectival/descriptive phrases joined by و: شادان‌دل 'cheerful/joyful-hearted' and تازه‌روی literally 'fresh-faced/new-faced' (poetic descriptor). Word order places the verb before these predicate/adverbial descriptions for a compact poetic line.
segments.s600-g111: بدانگه is a temporal adverb 'thereupon / at that moment'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/when', and شد is the past tense 'became' (from شدن). پادشاهیش is the nominal pādšāhī with a possessive/third-person marker (his kingship/royal rule) and راست here functions as a predicate adjective 'firm/true/established'. The clause literally reads 'thereupon that his kingship became firm.'
segments.s600-g112: فزون گشت is a verbal phrase where فزون 'increased/more' combines with گشت (past of گشتن/شدن 'became') to mean 'increased' or 'grew'. شادی is the noun 'joy' as the thing that increased. و links the clauses, and انده بکاست expresses the opposite: انده (here 'sorrow' / grief) and بکاست (past of کاستن with the prefix ب-, 'diminished') — so 'joy increased and sorrow decreased.' Poetic inversion leaves the predicate verb near the front for rhythm.
segments.s600-g73: برفتند is the past 3rd-person plural (they went/left). یکسر is an adverb placed after the verb in classical style meaning 'all at once' / 'together'. به is the preposition 'to' introducing the destination, and آتشکده is the object 'fire-temple'. Word order here is Verb – adverb – prepositional phrase (typical in poetry).
segments.s600-g74: A simple prepositional phrase: به 'to' followed by two coordinated place-nouns. ایوان نوروز is 'the iwan (porch/hall) of Nowruz' (compound), و joins the next noun, جشن سده is 'the Sadeh celebration' (سده here names the festival). The preposition applies to both coordinated nouns.
segments.s600-g75: همی is a classical/poetic adverbial particle indicating continued or habitual action in narration ('they then / they kept on'). مشک is the direct object 'musk'; بر + آتش is the prepositional phrase 'upon the fire'; افشاندند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they scattered/sprinkled'. Note the order: particle + object + prepositional phrase + verb (typical flexibility in older Persian).
segments.s600-g76: به بهرام is a dative prepositional phrase 'to Bahram'. بر آفرین خواندند is an idiomatic sequence: آفرین is an interjection 'bravo/praise', بر here gives direction or emphasis toward that acclamation, and خواندند (past 3pl) means 'they uttered/sang/called' — so 'they cried out “afarin” to Bahram.'
segments.s600-g77: وزان پس is a two-part connective: وزان 'and from that' (or 'thereupon') + پس 'then'. بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/said.' کارآگهان (plural) denotes 'the informed ones' or agents (here 'messengers'/'men who know the affairs') and is the indirect object/recipients of the order.
segments.s600-g78: یکی is 'one' (a single person). تا introduces a purposive clause here ('so that / in order that') and governs the subjunctive-like form بگردند (3rd pl subjunctive/present used for purpose: 'may go/roam'). گرد جهان is a fixed expression 'around the world' with گرد 'around' and جهان 'world.'
segments.s600-g79: کسی را uses کسی 'someone' with the definite/object marker را (classical Persian often marks the patient even in questions). کجا is the interrogative/adverb 'where'. رانده is the past participle/past form 'driven (away)' and is governed by the following agent phrase بد یزدگرد 'evil/wicked Yazdegerd' — together asking 'where had wicked Yazdegerd driven someone?'.
segments.s600-g80: بجست is a past verb here meaning 'he searched/sought' (classical past). و is the conjunction 'and'. به یک شهرشان is the prepositional phrase 'to one of their cities' (شهر + possessive شان 'their'). کرد + گرد is a light-verb construction: کرد 'did/made' with گرد 'around' giving the sense 'made his rounds / went about' or more simply 'went to' — a compact classical phrasing meaning he proceeded to that city.
segments.s600-g81: بدان here is a fused/archaic sequence (ب + دان) introducing purpose or result; followed by تا شود, a subordinate purpose clause: تا + شود (subjunctive/optative 'may become' or 'may be made'). نامه شهریار is a genitive phrase ('the king's letter') functioning as the thing that is to come into being. Word order is verb/auxiliary after تا, then the noun phrase (compound of نامه + شهریار).
segments.s600-g82: که introduces the content of that letter ('that / in which'); آزادگان را is a definite/direct-object/topic phrase marked by را; کند is the classical present/past auxiliary 'he makes/does' (here part of the light-verb construction with خواستار), and خواستار is used adjectivally/nominatively meaning 'requesting/asking for' — together خواستار کردن = 'to request,' so آزادگان را کند خواستار = 'he asks for the freemen.'
segments.s600-g83: فرستاد is a simple past 'he sent'; خلعت is the direct object 'robes of honour' (a customary gift); به هر مهتری is a prepositional dative ('to each chief') with هر 'each' modifying مهتری 'chief/elder'. The verb precedes the indirect-object phrase here (typical Persian order: verb — object — indirect phrase).
segments.s600-g84: ببخشید is past tense 'he bestowed/gave'; the following prepositional phrase به اندازه‌شان کشوری gives the beneficiary and measure: به + اندازه‌شان ('according to their measure/rank' with the possessive suffix -شان) and کشوری ('a realm/country', here used as the thing bestowed). The whole phrase means 'he bestowed a country on them in accordance with their rank.'
segments.s600-g85: This is a coordinated subject phrase: three nouns joined by و (and) — رد, موبد, مرزبان — followed by هرک 'whoever' and the copula بود 'was'. The sequence names officials and then uses هرک بود to mean 'whoever was (among them)'. The copula بود places the list in the past tense/background.
segments.s600-g86: که introduces the relative/subordinate clause describing those people: آواز بهرام 'Bahram's voice' is the object/theme, زان سان is an adverbial phrase ('from there / in that way' — زان = 'from that/there', سان = 'so/like'), and شنود is a classical third-person verbal form meaning 'hears/heard' (here narrative past). So the clause says they 'heard Bahram's voice from there in that manner.'
segments.s600-g87: سراسر is an adverb meaning 'all/throughout' placed sentence-initially; به درگاه شاه is the goal phrase ('to the king's court', where درگاه شاه = 'the royal presence/court' with the genitive relationship). آمدند is the past plural 'they came.' The order is adverb → prepositional phrase → verb (standard Persian ordering for emphasis).
segments.s600-g88: گشاده‌دل و نیکخواه are two predicative adjectives/epithets ('generous and well-wishing') joined by و; they are followed by آمدند (again) which acts as the verb applying to both epithets — a stylistic repetition for emphasis: 'they came generous and well-disposed.'
segments.s600-g57: کجا is the interrogative adverb 'where'. بود is the past 3rd-person singular of بودن ('was'), so the clause literally asks 'where was...'. دانا is the predicate noun 'a wise one' following the verb (classical word order places the predicate after بود). بدان روزگار is a single temporal phrase 'in that age' formed from the prepositional prefix ب- + the demonstrative/old-form دان + روزگار; together they locate the time.
segments.s600-g58: شمار جهان داشت is a verb phrase: شمار 'a tally/count' with جهان 'of the world' in apposition, and داشت (past 3rd-singular of داشتن) 'had/kept'. اندر کنار is a locative phrase 'at (or by) the side' where اندر is the preposition 'in/within' and کنار 'side/beside' shows the possession was kept at his side.
segments.s600-g59: جوانوی بیدار is a noun + adjective compound: جوانوی ('young man' / 'youthful') modified by بیدار 'awake, alert'. با او بهم means 'together with him' (با 'with' + او 'him' + بهم 'together/in company'); the order is emphatic/poetic but functions as 'he was with him'.
segments.s600-g60: که introduces a relative clause ('who'). نزدیک او is an adverbial phrase 'near him' (نزدیک 'near' + او 'him'). بد شمار is an adjectival description where بد 'bad/ill' modifies شمار 'counting/tally' to mean 'bad at counting' or 'who counted poorly'. درم is the object being counted ('dirham(s)').
segments.s600-g61: ز باقی is 'from the rest' (ز a poetic 'from', باقی 'the remainder/the rest'). که introduces a relative clause 'who'. بد نزد ایرانیان is a predicate phrase meaning 'bad in the sight of the Iranians' where بد is the predicative adjective and نزد ایرانیان gives the standpoint ('in the view/presence of the Iranians').
segments.s600-g62: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past verb 'he ordered/said'. تا here introduces the purpose/result clause 'that ...' (i.e., 'that they should'). بگسلد is the verbal infinitive/subjunctive with the be- prefix meaning 'be broken/severed' or 'be separated'. از میان is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from among (them)'.
segments.s600-g63: دبیران دانا is a noun phrase 'the learned/wise secretaries' (دبیران 'secretaries' + دانا 'learned, wise'). به دیوان شدند uses به + دیوان 'to the divan/court' plus شدند (past 3rd-plural of شدن); in classical usage شد(ن) can indicate movement/arrival here 'they went/arrived at the divan'.
segments.s600-g64: ز بهر is a fused prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of' (ز + بهر). درم is the object 'dirhams' (i.e., the motive or purpose). پیش کیوان شدند = 'they went/presented themselves before Keyvān' where پیش 'before/in the presence of' + کیوان (a proper name here) and شدند again marks their arrival/presentation.
segments.s600-g97: ز گیتی به یزدان پناهید و بس is an instruction: ز گیتی (lit. 'from the world') is a prepositional phrase marking what one should flee; به یزدان ('to/into God') marks the object/destination of refuge. پناهید is a poetic imperative/hortative (2nd‑person plural or polite form) 'take refuge/seek shelter'; و بس is a postpositive remark 'and that is enough' (i.e. ‘that suffices’). Word order (preposition + object before verb) and the clipped conjunction give the line a brisk, poetic tone.
segments.s600-g98: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('for/that'); دارنده is a predicative noun 'possessor/sustainer'. اویست here functions as a fused pronoun + copula (poetic = او + است) meaning 'he is' (i.e. 'He is its possessor'); و فریادرس adds a second predicate 'and (a) succourer/rescuer'. The clause therefore explains why refuge in God suffices.
segments.s600-g99: هرانکس که is a standard relative phrase 'whoever who/that'; بگزید (from گزیدن) here means 'chooses/accepts' (a general, timeless action — classical/persuasive present sense) and governs فرمان ما 'our command' as its object. The clause names the person bound by what follows.
segments.s600-g100: نپیچد is a negative jussive/subjunctive 3rd‑person form ('let him not turn' / 'should not swerve'); سر ('head') is used idiomatically/metonymically for one's disposition/loyalty. از رای و پیمان ما is the prepositional phrase 'from our counsel and covenant' functioning as the complement of نپیچد: 'do not deviate from our plan and pledge.'
segments.s600-g101: برو is the imperative 'go' or 'go on' addressing the listener. نیکویها (plural) is the direct object 'good deeds/virtues' placed before the verb phrase. برافزون کنیم is a hortatory/first‑person plural construction ('let us make many / let us increase' or 'we will increase'), where برافزون is the verbal/adjectival stem 'make abundant' and کنیم (from کردن) is the auxiliary in the subjunctive/hortative ('let us do').
segments.s600-g102: ز دل is a prepositional phrase 'from the heart'; کینه و آز are the two things to be removed ('hatred/grudge' and 'greed'). بیرون کنیم ('let us put out / we will drive out') is another first‑person plural hortatory/subjunctive combining the adverb بیرون ('out') with کنیم (let us do). The clause means 'let us root hatred and greed out of the heart.'
segments.s600-g103: هرانکس که 'whoever' begins the conditional/relative clause; از داد بگریزد uses از + داد ('from justice') plus بگریزد (3rd‑person subjunctive/present of گریختن) meaning 'flees/escapes from'. اوی is an emphatic third‑person pronoun 'he/that one' referring back to the person described. The clause sets up the fate of that person.
segments.s600-g104: به بادآفره در بیاویزد is the main clause describing the consequence: به introduces the direction/effect ('into/by'); بادآفره is a compound adjective 'wind‑creating' or 'stormy' (personified power of wind); در here means 'into/within' (not 'door'); بیاویزد (poetic) is a 3rd‑person singular verbal form meaning 'will cast / will hang' (literally 'sets upon' or 'hangs on'). The final اوی again repeats/marks the pronoun 'him' as the object — together: 'the wind‑maker will cast him into the wind.'
segments.s600-g9: ز (a poetic preposition) + بس together mean “because of so much / on account of” (ز = because of here). زشت modifies both گفتار و کردار (“vile speech and deeds”). اوی is an archaic third-person possessive/enclitic placed after the noun phrase (’his’). Word order is typical older Persian: prepositional cause → adjectival phrase → possessed nouns.
segments.s600-g10: Another causal phrase introduced by ز “because of.” The nouns are listed in a series (بیدادی و درد و آزار) describing different kinds of harm; اوی again is the possessive ‘his’. The conjunction و links the nouns; the overall structure is parallel to the previous segment.
segments.s600-g11: دل ما is the subject (‘our heart’). به بهرام is a prepositional phrase ‘toward Bahram’ (به = towards/having regard to). ازان (‘from that’) gives the cause/result and is followed by بود (past copula) + predicate سرد (‘cold’). Word order places the causative adverbial (ازان) immediately before the verb.
segments.s600-g12: که introduces the explanatory clause (‘for / because’). از شاه means ‘because of the king’ (از = because of here). بودیم is the plural past copula ‘we were.’ یکسر (‘entirely’) modifies the predicate, and به درد is a predicative phrase meaning ‘in pain/afflicted.’ The clause gives the reason why their hearts were cold.
segments.s600-g13: بشد is an older/periphrastic past meaning ‘it happened / he came’ (here ‘Munḏir arrived’). The proper name منذر follows the verb (common poetic inversion). شاه را is the definite object marked by را (the king as object), and کرد + نرم is a causative construction ‘made (him) gentle/softened (him)’ with کرد functioning as ‘caused/made’ and نرم as the result adjective.
segments.s600-g14: بگسترد is a transitive verb ‘he spread / he uttered’ (past). پیشش = ‘before him / in his presence’ (prepositional/adverbial), and سخنهای گرم is the object (‘fervent/warm speeches/words’); سخنهای is the plural/possessive form of سخن and گرم describes their tone. The subject is left implicit (Munḏir).
segments.s600-g15: ببخشید is a past-tense verb ‘he forgave / he pardoned.’ اگر introduces a concessive/conditional (‘if / even if’). چندشان = ‘some of them’ (object/partitive), followed by بد گناه ‘bad/guilty of sin’ (an adjectival + noun phrase describing those people). The clause means he pardoned them despite some being sinful.
segments.s600-g16: که gives the reason (‘for / because’). با = ‘with / possessing’; گوهر here reads as inner worth/essence (’nobility’ or ‘noble nature’) rather than a literal jewel, and دادگر is an adjective/noun ‘just, righteous.’ بود شاه is the copular clause ‘the king was…’ with the qualities listed before the copula. Thus the segment justifies the pardon by citing the king’s noble and just character.
segments.s600-g89: بفرمود is a past, honorific/perfect verb 'he commanded/said' placed before the subordinate marker تا. تا here introduces the content of the command ('that/so that'). هرک بد دادجوی is the noun phrase inside that clause: هرک 'whoever' (indefinite relative), بد modifies دادجوی ('a bad/malicious claimant' — adjective before noun, a common older order).
segments.s600-g90: سوی introduces direction 'toward(s)/to'. موبد موبد is a doubled title/appellative (the priest, here emphasized); آورد is the past tense 'brought' and روی literally 'face' used idiomatically with آورد ('آوردِ روی' = 'bring into someone's presence' / 'bring before'). Word order places the directional phrase before the verb.
segments.s600-g91: چو ... آمد is a temporal clause 'when ... came'. فرمانش is 'his command/order' with enclitic possessive -ش. ز گیتی به جای is a poetic locution with ز = 'from' and به جای = 'to the place' (here signaling the arrival/coming into effect of the order; literary phrasing rather than a literal movement).
segments.s600-g92: منادیگری ('heralding' / the office or act of a herald) is the object/concept and کرد is the past tense 'did/made' — together 'he performed/appointed heralding'. بر در is a locative phrase 'at the gate/door' (در = gate). به پای here indicates position 'at the foot/standing at' and gives the sense 'standing at the gate' rather than movement.
segments.s600-g93: که‌ای here functions as a vocative interjection 'O' addressing the addressees (an archaic/poetic usage). زیردستان بیدار شاه is a noun phrase 'the king's wakeful/alert subjects' — بیدار qualifies زیردستان and شاه follows as the possessor (poetic word order: subject + modifier + possessor).
segments.s600-g94: ز غم دور باشید is an imperative/hortative clause: ز = 'from', غم = 'sorrow', دور باشید = 'be distant/keep away' (باشید is 2nd‑person plural/formal imperative). و links the two admonitions; دور از گناه repeats the idea 'be far from sin' (دور + از + گناه = 'remote from sin').
segments.s600-g95: وزین پس is a literary/time phrase meaning 'henceforth/from this time on' (وزین here is folded with پس; the lexical entry listed is not a perfect match, so the token is treated as a fixed phrase). بران کس کنید آفرین is an exhortation: بران کس 'those people' (بران is a demonstrative/relational particle here) + کنید (2nd‑person plural imperative 'do/make') + آفرین 'acclamation/praise' — 'give praise to those people.'
segments.s600-g96: که introduces the reason/explanation 'for/that'. از marks source/means 'by/from' and داد here means 'justice' (abstract noun). آباد is a predicative/adjectival element 'prosperous' and دارد is the present/past auxiliary 'has/keeps' — the clause literally reads 'for from justice the land has prosperity' i.e. 'for by justice the land is made prosperous.' زمین is the theme 'land/earth' placed at the end in poetic order.
segments.s600-g65: ز is the preposition 'from'. باقی is the noun 'the rest/remaining (ones)'. که is a relative pronoun 'that/who' introducing a descriptive clause. بد is an adjective 'bad/wicked' modifying باقی, and the prepositional phrase بر جهان سربسر ('upon the world, entirely') is a poetic adverbial that scopes the adjective; word order (adjective + prepositional phrase after کہ) is typical of Ferdowsi's poetic inversion.
segments.s600-g66: همه is the subject 'all/everyone'. برگرفتند is 3rd-person plural past (they took/seized); in this context it carries the sense 'seized/assailed' (they fell upon). یک با دگر is a reciprocal phrase 'one with the other/one another' (یک = one, با = with, دگر = the other). The verb follows the subject as usual in past-tense narrative.
segments.s600-g67: نود بار و سه بار is a poetic way to express 'ninety and three times' (i.e. ninety-three times); بار here means 'time/occasion'. کرده is a past participle 'having done' and شمار is 'count/number' — together the phrase indicates 'having made the count' (they counted). The sequence places the numerals and repeated 'بار' for emphasis, a common stylistic device.
segments.s600-g68: به ایران is a prepositional phrase 'to/for Iran' (recipient). درم is the direct noun 'dirhams' (money). هزاران هزار is a poetic intensifier meaning 'thousands upon thousands'. The main verb expressing 'gave' or similar is implied by the surrounding narrative (the verb can be elliptic in poetry); the word بد appears here in the manuscript text but does not match the senses listed (no 'gave' sense is provided), so the action of giving is understood from context rather than from a supplied verb form.
segments.s600-g69: ببخشید is 3rd-person plural past 'they forgave' (here meaning 'they pardoned' or cancelled obligations). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. دیوان in this context is 'the register/records' (the tax/office rolls). بر آتش نهاد is a compound phrase: بر = 'on/upon', آتش = 'fire', نهاد = past 'placed/put' — together 'placed upon the fire' (i.e. they burned the records). The subject 'they' is implied and applies to both verbs.
segments.s600-g70: همه شهر ایران is a nominal phrase 'the whole city (or all the city) of Iran' acting as subject. بدو is the dative/clitic pronoun 'to him/for him' (referring back to the benefactor), گشت is past 'became/turned', and شاد is the adjective 'happy/merry'. The sequence is subject + dative + verb + adjective: 'the whole city of Iran became happy for him.'
segments.s600-g71: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاه شد is a two-word predicate 'became aware' (past tense). زان سخن is a genitival/oblique phrase 'of that speech/news' (زان = 'of/from that', سخن = 'speech/news'). هرکسی is the subject 'each person/everyone' placed at the end for poetic emphasis; the clause reads 'when each person became aware of that news.'
segments.s600-g72: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle 'continually/kept on'. آفرین here is the interjection 'Bravo!' or 'well done'. خواند is past 'uttered/called' (here 'cried/sang out'); هرکس 'each person/everyone' again as subject; بسی is an adverb 'much/a great deal' (intensive). Together: 'each person kept crying “Bravo!” many times.'
segments.s600-g33: This segment is a coordinated list introduced by the preposition به (here functioning like 'with/for' in a poetic register) that enumerates the martial items used to equip or adorn: اسپ (horse) and سنان (spear), then another به introduces خفتان (a war‑related word here in a poetic/archaised usage) together with جنگ (war/battle) as the domain or type of gear. The verb that completes the idea is not inside this segment but the prepositional phrase functions as the set of items given/used.
segments.s600-g34: ز (poetic 'from') marks source/means: ز خود means 'from his own' (خود as possessive 'his own'), و links the phrases, ز هر گوهری = 'from every jewel' (هر = 'every'), and رنگ‑رنگ is a reduplicated adjective placed after the noun meaning 'variously colored/multicolored'. Together this phrase indicates that jewels of many colors, including his own, were used to ornament or constitute the gifts.
segments.s600-g35: سراسر is an adverb meaning 'throughout/entirely' placed before the indirect object phrase; به + personal names (نعمان و منذر) marks the recipients 'to Nuʿmān and Munḏir'. سپرد is the past tense verb 'entrusted/handed over' (3rd person past), so the clause reads '(he) entrusted wholly to Nuʿmān and Munḏir.'
segments.s600-g36: جوانوی here denotes 'a youth/young man' (subject) and precedes the past verb رفت ('went/left', 3rd person). The second clause آن بدیشان شمرد uses آن as the direct object 'that/those (items)', بدیشان = به + ایشان 'to them', and شمرد is past 'he counted' or 'he reckoned (out)'; syntactically the two verbs relate to the same agent: 'a young man went and counted those to them.'
segments.s600-g37: کس here functions in the negative sense 'no one' when combined with the negative verb that follows. اندازه بخشش is a noun phrase 'the measure/extent of his giving/generosity' (اندازه = measure/degree, بخشش = gift/generosity). نداشت is past negative 'did not have/possess', so the clause says 'no one had the extent/measure of his generosity.'
segments.s600-g38: همان is a demonstrative 'that same' (pointing back to the foregoing quality), تاو is a poetic noun 'radiance/glow' (here used figuratively), and با کوشش او is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with his effort' (با = with, کوشش = effort, او = his). نداشت is again past negative 'did not have', so the literal structure is 'that very radiance did not have (exist) with his effort' (poetically indicating a link between glory/radiance and exertion).
segments.s600-g39: همان here identifies 'those' (the previously mentioned people), تازیان is the definite plural 'the Arabs', and را is the direct‑object marker. بسی (here adverbial) modifies هدیه 'gift' to indicate largeness/abundance, and داد is the past verb 'gave'. The clause therefore states 'he gave those Arabs many gifts.'
segments.s600-g40: از ایوان شاهی is a prepositional phrase 'from the royal portico' (از = from, ایوان = portico/veranda, شاهی = royal), برفتند is past plural 'they departed/went away', and شاد is a predicative/adverbial adjective 'happy/joyful'. Word order places source first followed by the verb and the accompanying manner/state: 'they left the royal portico joyful.'
segments.s600-g17: بیاراست here is a past/adjectival perfect used in classical Persian meaning ‘adorned’ (literally ‘made beautiful’). Word order is verb + noun phrase: بیاراست governs ایوان شاهنشهی, where ایوان (noun) is followed by its attributive شاهنشهی (‘imperial/kingly’).
segments.s600-g18: آنک is the archaic relative pronoun آن‌که (‘those/one who’). برفت is an older past form meaning ‘went up/entered’ (prefixed بر‑ + رفتن) and here links to the relative phrase آنک بودند یکسر مهی as its subject; بودند is the past plural ‘they were’. یکسر is an adverb ‘entirely/wholly’ modifying مهی, a poetic adjective ‘moon‑like / beautiful (like the moon)’. The clause thus says ‘those who were entirely moon‑like went up.’
segments.s600-g19: چو here functions as the temporal conjunction ‘when’. جای بزرگی is ‘the great places/seats’ (جای = place/seat; بزرگی qualifies it as grand). بپرداختند is the past plural ‘they arranged/attended to’ (پرداختن in the sense ‘set in order’), so the clause reads ‘when they arranged the great seats…’.
segments.s600-g20: کرا is an archaic interrogative/relative meaning ‘who/which’ or ‘whoever’. بود is the past copula ‘was/were’ used here in the sense ‘it was (so)’. شایسته means ‘appropriate/worthy’, and بنشاختند is past plural ‘they sat down / took their seats’ (classical form for نشستن). Together: ‘whoever was worthy sat down (took his place).’
segments.s600-g21: به هر جای خوانی: به…بیاراستند frames a locative/frame for the banquet: به هر = ‘at every/each’, جای = ‘place/seat’, and خوانی here functions as ‘(place) of the feast/banquet’ (classical خوان ‘banquet/table’). بیاراستند is the past plural ‘they adorned/arranged’. The whole means ‘they arranged (or adorned) every seat at the table.’
segments.s600-g22: This is a coordination with verb خواستند (past plural ‘they requested/they called for’). می‌و (mi-o) stands for wine/banquet produce (poetically ‘wine/fruit’), رود here names musical sound/instrument (contextually ‘music’ or a musical instrument), و is ‘and’, رامشگران are the performers/entertainers (here people who provide comfort/pleasantness through music), and خواستند is ‘they summoned/desired’. The clause lists what they ordered for the feast: wine, music, and entertainers.
segments.s600-g23: This is a temporal clause: دوم روز (second day) + رفتند ‘they went/left’ (past plural). دیگر گروه = ‘the other/the remaining group/company’. The clause thus reads ‘on the second day the other company left.’
segments.s600-g24: سپهبد is the title ‘spahbed’ (a high military commander). نیامد is the negative past ‘did not come’. ز (زی) is the poetic preposition ‘from/because of’, introducing the cause: خوردن ‘eating’ (here ‘to eat’ / ‘feasting’) and ستوه ‘weary/tired of’. The clause therefore means ‘the spahbed did not come, being weary/sated from eating.’
segments.s600-g25: سیم روز (si-yom ruz) is a poetic order meaning "the third day" (سِیّم/سیّم روز). The clause then lists festive elements (جشن و می‌و سور) before the copula بود, so word order is topical: time → list of festivities → past copula 'was'. بود is past tense 'was'.
segments.s600-g26: This is a short nominal clause with غم as subject and دور بود as the predicate: 'sorrow was distant.' از کاخ شاه جهان places the location/source in front of the adjective دور (literally 'from the palace of the king of the world it was distant'), a common poetic word order.
segments.s600-g27: بگفت is the past tense ('he said'). آنک functions as a relative/connector 'that/which' introducing the subordinate content clause. نعمان و منذر are the coordinated subjects inside the indirect question clause چه کرد 'what did (they) do' — here reporting 'what they had done.'
segments.s600-g28: ز بهر is a fixed poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'for/on account of/for the sake of'; من is the object 'me.' این پاک‌زاده دو مرد is an appositive noun phrase 'these two noble-born men' identifying the actors; note the demonstrative این and the noun پاک‌زاده (adjective‑like compound) follow the prepositional phrase in typical Persian order.
segments.s600-g29: همه مهتران is the plural subject 'all the chiefs/elders' (poetic). خواندند is the past plural verb 'they cried/sang/called out' and takes آفرین as its object/interjection 'bravo' or 'well done.' Word order is subject → verb → object in past tense.
segments.s600-g30: بران is a demonstrative/adverbial 'upon that' or 'to that.' دشت آباد is a compound place-name phrase 'prosperous plain' and مردان کین is a genitive phrase 'men of vengeance' (heroes motivated by vengeance). Together they indicate the recipients/targets of the acclamation.
segments.s600-g31: ازان پس is an adverbial phrase 'after that.' در گنج literally 'in the treasure/treasury' marks the locale/object of the forthcoming action. The verb بگشاد is a past form 'opened' (verb before subject in this poetic order), with شاه following as the subject: 'the king opened (the treasury).'
segments.s600-g32: به دینار و دیبا is an instrumental/prepositional phrase 'with dinars and brocade' (what he used or distributed). بیاراست is the past of آراستن with the old prefixed form (he adorned/arrayed), and گاه here refers to the place/occasion that was adorned — poetic placement puts the adverbial noun after the verb.
segments.s600-g1: دگر روز is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘the next/another day.’ چون here is a subordinating conjunction (‘when/as’) introducing a temporal clause. بردمید آفتاب is a poetic past verbal phrase: بردمید is an older/poetic 3rd-person past verb meaning ‘rose/appeared’ and the subject آفتاب follows it (V S order is common in classical Persian poetry).
segments.s600-g2: This clause contains two coordinated past verbs whose understood subject is کوه. ببالید is a past-form verb meaning ‘lifted itself / reared / grew proud’ (the mountain ‘rose’), followed by the subject کوه; و coordinates the next verb بپالود, which takes خواب (‘sleep’) as its object — i.e. the mountain also ‘shook off / cleared’ sleep. The verbs are classical/poetic past forms with the subject either after the first verb or understood for both verbs.
segments.s600-g3: به نزدیک منذر is a prepositional/adverbial phrase ‘to/near Munḏir’ placed before the verb. شدند is the past 3rd-person-plural verb ‘they came / became / arrived’ and the subject این گروه (‘this group/party’) appears after the verb (typical classical word order: adjunct — verb — subject).
segments.s600-g4: که introduces a clause giving the reason or background (‘for/because’). بهرام شه is a noun phrase ‘Bahram, the king’ (name + title). بود is the past copula ‘was,’ and زیشان is the preposition ز + the clitic شان meaning ‘from/of them / in respect to them’; ستوه is a predicate adjective ‘weary/annoyed.’ The literal order is copula + prepositional phrase + adjective: ‘was (از them) weary.’
segments.s600-g5: که here links this instruction to what was said before (introducing the content of their counsel). خواهشگری is a verbal noun meaning ‘entreaty/pleading’; کن is the imperative ‘do/make’ (i.e., ‘make a plea’). به نزدیک شاه is the goal phrase ‘to the presence of the king / before the king’ with نزدیک functioning as a locative/adverbial.
segments.s600-g6: ز کردار ما is a prepositional phrase (‘because of / on account of our conduct’) giving the matter of the plea. تا introduces a purpose/result clause (‘so that / in order that’). ببخشد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive (classical form with the ب- prefix) meaning ‘may forgive’ and گناه is the object ‘sin/fault’; together the clause expresses the intended result: ‘so that he may forgive the sin.’
segments.s600-g7: که again introduces an explanatory clause. چونان is a modifier meaning ‘such / so (like that).’ بدیم here is a nominalized/poetic use of بد meaning ‘evilness / something bad (we experienced).’ از بد یزدگرد means ‘from the evil Yazdegerd’ (از = from, بد = wicked/bad, یزدگرد = the king’s name). The phrase says they had seen such evil originating with Yazdegerd.
segments.s600-g8: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause about Yazdegerd. خون is the grammatical subject ‘blood,’ در تن is a locative phrase ‘in the body,’ and نامداران (in the genitive relation to تن) specifies whose bodies: ‘the renowned / notable men.’ فسرد is a past verb meaning ‘cooled / chilled / froze’ (poetic/archaic form), so the clause literally says ‘the blood in the bodies of the famed was chilled’ — i.e. he put terror into them.
segments.s600-g49: ورا is the archaic object pronoun (= او را) placed before the predicate for emphasis; پهلوان is the noun complement 'champion/hero'; کرد is the past-tense verb 'made/appointed' (3rd sg.). بر لشکرش is a prepositional phrase 'over/against his army' (بر = over, لشکرِش = 'his army'), giving the scope of the appointment.
segments.s600-g50: بدان تا is a classical connective meaning roughly 'thereby/so that' or 'with the result that' (بدان an archaic compound; تا introduces the result/purpose clause). به آیین بود کشورش is a copular clause: به آیین = 'according to custom/rite' (به as a relational preposition), بود is the past copula 'was/should be', and کشورش 'his realm' (کشور + enclitic ش = 'his').
segments.s600-g51: سپه را uses سپه 'army' with the object marker را to mark the direct object; سراسر is an adverb/adjective 'entire/throughout' modifying the transfer; به + Nersi indicates the recipient ('to Nersi' — here Nersi is a proper name); سپرد is the past verb 'entrusted/committed' (3rd sg.). The clause is V-final with the object fronted by را, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s600-g52: به بخشش here functions instrumentally 'by/through (the) giving' (به = by), بخشش = 'gift/grant/generosity'; همی is a classical pre-verbal particle that marks continuous/habitual or potential sense (here giving the sense 'would'/'would thus'); پادشاهی ببرد is the verb phrase 'win/gain kingship' where ببرد (classical present/potential form) expresses the result 'he would gain' (ببرد used in narration to indicate obtaining/winning).
segments.s600-g53: در گنج is the prepositional object 'in/into the treasure (treasury)'; بگشاد is an archaic past form 'opened' (b‑prefix + verb); و links clauses; روزی بداد is an idiom 'he gave sustenance/provisions' (روزی = provisions/sustenance, بداد = past 'gave' — here the b‑form of دادن used in older Persian).
segments.s600-g54: سپاهش 'his army' is the subject (سپاه + enclitic ش = 'his'); به دینار is an instrumental/cause phrase 'with dinars/for dinars' (به used to mark the means); گشتند is the past plural 'they became' (گشتن used causatively/become); شاد is the predicative adjective 'happy/joyful'.
segments.s600-g55: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said'; پس 'then' sequences the narrative; تا here introduces the subordinate clause (functioning as 'until' / 'that/so that' depending on context); گشسپ دبیر is a name plus title in apposition 'Goshasp, the secretary/scribe' and serves as the subject of the following verb.
segments.s600-g56: بیامد is the past intransitive 'he came'; بر شاه is the prepositional complement 'to the king' (بر = toward/to in this context); مردم پذیر is a postpositive adjectival phrase 'people‑receiving' (مردم = people, پذیر = receptive/accepting) describing the role/purpose (i.e. to receive the people). The subject (Goshasp) is understood from the previous segment.
segments.s600-g41: بیاورد is a 3rd-person past/perfective narrative verb 'he/they brought'; the subject is omitted (typical epic narrative). پس is a temporal adverb 'then' that follows the verb here. خلعت خسروی is a noun phrase where خلعت 'robe of honour' is qualified by the adjective/attributive خسروی 'royal/kingly' placed after it.
segments.s600-g42: همان اسپ is 'that very steed' (همان = 'that/the very'); و هم links items 'and also'; جامه پهلوی is 'a Pahlavi (i.e. Pahlavi/Parthian‑style) garment' with پهلوی functioning as a cultural/style adjective after جامه. Word order (noun + adjective) and the doubled particle هم give emphasis and listing.
segments.s600-g43: به خسرو سپردند is 'they handed (it/them) to Khosrow' — سپردند is 3rd‑person plural past 'they entrusted/handed over' and به marks the indirect object. و بنواختش coordinates another action: بنواختش (here) means 'they struck/played (music) for him' or 'they sounded (a fanfare)'; the -ش suffix refers back to the person/thing acted on.
segments.s600-g44: بر گاه فرخنده is a prepositional phrase 'on/onto the auspicious dais/platform' (بر = on; گاه here = place/occasion, and فرخنده = 'auspicious, fortunate' qualifies it). بنشاختش is 3rd‑person plural past 'they placed/they set him' with the clitic -ش = 'him', completing the action of seating.
segments.s600-g45: شهنشاه خسرو is the title + name 'King (Emperor) Khosrow'. به ... رسید is a verbal phrase 'reached/arrived at ...' where به marks the goal or point reached; نرسی here functions as the goal/place/person reached and is followed by the verb رسید 'arrived' in the past (3rd singular).
segments.s600-g46: ز تخت اندر آمد is a compact clause 'he came down/from the throne' (ز = from; تخت = throne; اندر آمد = 'came in/descended'). به کرسی رسید is 'he reached/arrived at the seat' (به marks the destination; کرسی = seat/throne; رسید is past 'arrived'). The two clauses describe movement from the throne to the seat.
segments.s600-g47: برادرش is 'his brother' (برادر + pronominal suffix -ش). بد here is an adjective 'bad/hostile' modifying the brother. یک‌دل و یک‌زبان are compound adjectives 'of one heart and one tongue' (i.e. united in purpose/speech); they are coordinated with و and further characterize the brother's disposition toward the focal person.
segments.s600-g48: ازو کهتر is a comparative construction 'smaller/younger than him' where ازو ('than him') marks the standard of comparison and کهتر is the comparative adjective 'inferior/younger'. آن نامدار جوان is the noun phrase 'that renowned youth' — the phrase being compared (word order places the comparative before the noun in this poetic line).

sentences.s600-s57: Every day his hunting went poorly; besides, horses, the field, polo and the ball.
sentences.s600-s53: If now the Creator grants strength, may the times become to the wish of our hearts.
sentences.s600-s54: There was an increase in these good things; you received praise from us.
sentences.s600-s55: All the cities of Iran went at his word, joyful-hearted and fresh-faced.
sentences.s600-s56: When, at that time, his kingship became secure, joy increased and sorrow lessened.
sentences.s600-s37: They all went at once to the fire-temple, to the iwan of Nowruz and the Sadeh festivities.
sentences.s600-s38: They sprinkled musk on the fire and cried out praises to Bahram.
sentences.s600-s39: After that he commanded the informed men to have one go about the world.
sentences.s600-s40: He sought where wicked Yazdegerd had driven anyone, and (then) went to one of their cities.
sentences.s600-s41: So that a royal letter might be drawn up in which the king requests the freemen.
sentences.s600-s42: He sent robes of honour to each chief and bestowed upon them a realm according to their rank.
sentences.s600-s43: Every judge, mobad, and frontier-warden who was there heard Bahram's voice from that place in such a manner.
sentences.s600-s44: They all came to the king's court; they arrived generous and well-disposed.
sentences.s600-s29: Where, in that age, was there a wise man who kept a reckoning of the world at his side?
sentences.s600-s30: A watchful young man was together with him, who, standing near him, miscounted the dirhams.
sentences.s600-s31: Of the rest, those who were bad in the view of the Iranians he commanded to be severed from their midst.
sentences.s600-s32: The learned secretaries went to the divan; for the sake of dirhams they presented themselves before Keyvān.
sentences.s600-s49: Seek refuge in God from the world — that is enough, for He is its possessor and helper.
sentences.s600-s50: Whoever accepts our command should not turn aside from our counsel and covenant.
sentences.s600-s51: Go forth; we will multiply good deeds, and we will drive hatred and greed out of the heart.
sentences.s600-s52: Whoever flees from justice — him (indeed) the wind‑creator will fling into the wind.
sentences.s600-s5: Because of his exceedingly vile speech and deeds, and because of his injustice, suffering, and persecution.
sentences.s600-s6: Our heart toward Bahram was cold from that, for we were entirely afflicted because of the king.
sentences.s600-s7: Munḏir arrived, and he softened the king; before him he spread fervent words.
sentences.s600-s8: He pardoned them — even if some of them were guilty of bad sins — for the king was noble in nature and just.
sentences.s600-s45: He commanded that whoever was a malicious claimant be brought before the mōbed.
sentences.s600-s46: When his order came, he set up a herald at the gate.
sentences.s600-s47: O watchful subjects of the king: be far from sorrow and far from sin.
sentences.s600-s48: From now on, praise those people, for by justice the land is made prosperous.
sentences.s600-s33: The rest — who were wicked throughout the world — all fell upon one another.
sentences.s600-s34: Having counted ninety and three times, they gave to Iran dirhams by the thousands.
sentences.s600-s35: They forgave and set the registers on the fire; the whole city of Iran rejoiced for him.
sentences.s600-s36: When each person learned that news, everyone repeatedly cried out 'Bravo!'
sentences.s600-s17: He outfitted (them) with horses and spears and with battle-gear, and adorned (them) with his own and with every jewel of varied colors.
sentences.s600-s18: He handed everything over to Nuʿmān and Munḏir; a young man went and counted them out to those (men).
sentences.s600-s19: No one possessed the measure of his generosity; that very radiance also did not have his effort.
sentences.s600-s20: He gave those Arabs many gifts; they departed from the royal portico rejoicing.
sentences.s600-s9: They adorned the kingly hall; those who were wholly moon‑like went up (into it).
sentences.s600-s10: When the great places were arranged, whoever was worthy took his seat.
sentences.s600-s11: They set every place at the table; they called for wine, music and entertainers.
sentences.s600-s12: On the second day the rest of the company departed; the spahbed did not come, being tired of eating.
sentences.s600-s13: On the third day there were celebrations, fruit and feasting; sorrow was far from the king's palace of the world.
sentences.s600-s14: He said what Nuʿmān and Munḏir had done for me, these two noble-born men.
sentences.s600-s15: All the chiefs cried 'Bravo!' to that prosperous plain and to the vengeful heroes.
sentences.s600-s16: After that the king opened the treasure and adorned the place with dinars and brocade.
sentences.s600-s1: The next day, when the sun rose, the mountain lifted itself and shook off sleep.
sentences.s600-s2: This party drew near Munḏir, for Bahram the king was weary of them.
sentences.s600-s3: So (make) a plea before the king — plead concerning our conduct so that he may forgive the sin.
sentences.s600-s4: for we had seen such wickedness from evil Yazdegerd — he who chilled the blood in the bodies of the famed.
sentences.s600-s25: He appointed him champion over his army so that his realm might be kept according to custom.
sentences.s600-s26: He handed the whole army over to Nersi; by gifts he would win the kingship.
sentences.s600-s27: He opened the treasury and distributed provisions; his army became glad at the dinars.
sentences.s600-s28: Then he ordered that Goshasp the scribe come to the king (to receive the people).
sentences.s600-s21: Then he brought the royal robe — that very steed and also a Pahlavi garment.
sentences.s600-s22: They handed (them) to Khosrow; amid music they set him upon the auspicious dais.
sentences.s600-s23: King Khosrow arrived at Narsi; he descended from the throne and took his place on the seat.
sentences.s600-s24: His brother, hostile and of one heart and one tongue toward him, was younger than that famed youth.
